caseName: Cullen v The State of New South Wales
oneLine: Police owe bystanders a duty of care when they move into a crowd to make an arrest — but a proportionate response to a real threat is not negligence, even when someone gets hurt.
whatHappened: On 26 January 2017, an “Invasion Day” protest march of about 5,000 people moved through inner Sydney. Laura CULLEN was watching from the crowd. As officers of the Operational Support Group moved in — and while Leading Senior Constable Livermore was arresting a marcher — a melee erupted and CULLEN was knocked to the ground and seriously injured. At trial she was awarded $800,000; the NSW Court of Appeal reversed that. She appealed to the High Court.
whatHeld: The appeal turned on three questions — duty, breach, and scope. Duty — yes: when intervening in the march, the officers owed a duty to avoid physical injury to a class of people that included bystanders foreseeably at risk from the operation. Breach — no: their actions were not unreasonable given the public-safety threat they were responding to at the time. With no breach, the scope question did not arise. Appeal dismissed.
whatItMeans: Read it for the principle, not the charge — this is NSW civil negligence, not WA criminal law, so it changes no offence you lay. But the principle is High Court and it travels: when you intervene operationally (a crowd, a public-order job, an arrest in a tight space) you owe bystanders a duty of care, and you are judged on the reasonableness of your response to the threat as it appeared at the time, not with hindsight. What saved the officers was a documented, proportionate plan (their “Operational Orders” and “Actions On”) — so record your threat assessment and why the response was proportionate (running sheet, notes, BWC). The same duty/breach reasoning runs through the Civil Liability Act 2002 (WA).
verdict: Useful for use-of-force and civil-liability exposure. It does not change how you investigate, gather evidence, or lay a charge.

court: Supreme Court of WA — Court of Appeal
outcome: Appeal dismissed — but on the facts (the schizophrenia was not shown to operate independently of the cannabis), NOT because voluntary intoxication is an absolute bar to ss 27/28.
weight: WA Court of Appeal — binds WA courts below
oneLine: Voluntary intoxication does NOT automatically defeat an unsoundness-of-mind defence: an accused can still rely on s 27 if a mental impairment deprived them of capacity independently of the intoxication — but not where the intoxication itself (or its effect on a condition) caused it. STEFANSKI lost only because his schizophrenia couldn't be separated from his cannabis use.
whatHappened: An appeal against conviction on guilty pleas to two counts of murder. The appellant argued he could not in law be guilty (or was not guilty by reason of unsoundness of mind) where a psychosis had been triggered by his voluntary cannabis use — turning on the construction of ss 27, 28(1) and 28(2) of the Criminal Code (WA). The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal. (Your instructor placed the facts at a ‘Greenwood’ murder; the judgment does not name Greenwood — the legal point and citation are what matter.)
whatHeld: The Court of Appeal (Buss P, Mazza JA, Beech JA) construed ss 27, 28(1) and 28(2) of the Criminal Code (WA) and held — rejecting the contrary view in Herbert as 'wrong' — that an accused who intentionally became intoxicated may still rely on s 27(1) if they prove, on the balance of probabilities, a 'mental impairment' that deprived them of a relevant capacity independently of and without regard to the intoxication. What s 28(2) bars is reliance on s 27 where the voluntary intoxication itself — or its effect in triggering or worsening a pre-existing condition — caused the loss of capacity. The appeal was dismissed on the facts: the psychiatric evidence could not show STEFANSKI's paranoid schizophrenia operated independently of his admitted cannabis intoxication, and his guilty pleas were a considered free choice (Meissner).
whatItMeans: The practical lesson survives, but the rule is narrower than the shorthand. Get bloods early — not because a voluntary intoxicant automatically defeats an insanity defence (it does not), but because proving what was in the system goes to the question that actually decides these cases: could the mental impairment have operated independently of the intoxicant? For any serious matter, secure a forensic blood sample before alcohol/cannabis/meth metabolises, so the psychiatric picture can be disentangled. Exam heads-up: the simplified line 'voluntary intoxication can't ground unsoundness of mind' overstates it — that is not what the Court held.
verdict: ACTION — drives an investigative step (early forensic bloods) and corrects a common overstatement of the intoxication/insanity rule.

court: Supreme Court of WA — Court of Appeal
caseName: Gibson v The State of Western Australia
outcome: Appeal allowed; manslaughter conviction (on a guilty plea) set aside; judgment of acquittal entered — no retrial.
weight: WA Court of Appeal — binds WA courts below
oneLine: GIBSON — a Pintupi man with significant cognitive impairment and very little English — pleaded guilty to a manslaughter he likely never understood he could fight. The plea lacked integrity, so the conviction was set aside and a judgment of acquittal entered.
whatHappened: Joshua WARNEKE was found dead beside Old Broome Road in February 2010. GIBSON — an 18-year-old Pintupi man from Kiwirrkurra, unable to read English and with significant cognitive impairment — was charged with murder in 2012. His records of interview were excluded at first instance (not voluntary; Criminal Investigation Act 2006 (WA) breaches; an interpreter plainly required). With the interviews gone, the case rested on companion witnesses whose accounts kept shifting. Instructions to plead were taken at two rushed conferences — one with no interpreter, one with an interpreter not accredited in his language — and GIBSON pleaded guilty to manslaughter, receiving 7 years 6 months. Official reviews later found the inculpatory witness statements had falsely implicated him.
whatHeld: The Court of Appeal allowed the appeal under s 30(3)(c) of the Criminal Appeals Act 2004 (WA): a conviction founded on a guilty plea will be set aside as a miscarriage of justice where the integrity of the plea is impugned — here because GIBSON's significant cognitive impairment and profound language disability meant he did not sufficiently understand the plea or the real choice between pleading guilty and standing trial (where an acquittal would mean release). The recognised categories for overturning a guilty plea are not closed (Vella), and the rule that a plea is a free choice by a person 'of sound mind and understanding' (Meissner) presupposes an understanding he did not have. Because the only inculpatory witnesses were now unreliable and could not properly be led — leaving no case to answer — the Court entered a judgment of acquittal rather than ordering a retrial (s 30(5)(b)).
whatItMeans: Your lessons sit in what went wrong, not the appeal point. Get the interview right: GIBSON's records of interview were thrown out for involuntariness and Criminal Investigation Act breaches, and once they were gone the case had no spine. For a vulnerable or Aboriginal suspect, use a properly accredited interpreter in the right language and a prisoner's friend, and explain the caution phrase by phrase — an untrained community member standing in as interpreter was not enough (read with Anunga). And don't shape your witnesses: the case collapsed because companion accounts shifted and 'critical details' had been introduced by interviewers — keep questions non-leading, corroborate independently, and keep investigating beyond the admission. (The 'reasonable suspicion / suspect status' point you may link to 'Gibson' is actually the separate first-instance ruling that excluded the interviews — The State of WA v Gibson [2014] WASC 240 — not this appeal.)
verdict: ACTION — a wrongful-conviction cautionary tale that drives interview discipline: lawful, voluntary, properly-interpreted records of interview, and witness evidence you have not contaminated.

weight: High Court — binds all Australian courts
oneLine: The High Court restored BADEN-CLAY's murder conviction, holding the Court of Appeal wrongly substituted manslaughter on an 'unintended killing' theory never run at trial and not open on the evidence. The modern authority on appellate restraint and inference-drawing in a circumstantial case.
whatHappened: Gerard BADEN-CLAY was convicted of murdering his wife, Allison. The case was circumstantial; his conduct and lies — including an affair and a claim that scratches on his neck were ‘from shaving’ — featured at trial. The Queensland Court of Appeal substituted manslaughter; the High Court restored the murder conviction, addressing how inferences may be drawn in a circumstantial case.
whatHeld: In a wholly circumstantial case the appellate court must respect the jury's function in drawing the available inferences (M v The Queen), and cannot quash a murder verdict and substitute manslaughter on a hypothesis — here, an unintended killing in a physical confrontation — that was not run at trial and not open on the evidence (the defence case had been that the accused had nothing to do with the death). The murder conviction was restored. The Court indicated it was unnecessary to rely on the accused's lies as consciousness of guilt to support the verdict.
whatItMeans: Read it for the inference-drawing, not as a 'lies' case. It is the leading modern statement that a properly directed jury's inferences in a circumstantial case are not lightly overturned — so build the circumstantial brief so the inferences you want the jury to draw are genuinely open on the evidence. (Still useful alongside Edwards on lies/conduct — but the High Court did not decide it on that ground.)
verdict: AWARENESS — the High Court authority on appellate restraint and inference in circumstantial cases; know how it actually reasoned, not the 'lies worked example' shorthand.

court: Supreme Court of New South Wales
outcome: Magistrate's dismissals of the assault, resist and intimidate police charges quashed; matters remitted to the magistrate to be dealt with according to law, principally because the prosecution was denied procedural fairness over material the magistrate researched himself.
weight: Single-judge decision of the Supreme Court of NSW (SMART AJ) on a s.109(a) Justices Act 1902 review; reported at 127 A Crim R 151. Persuasive and frequently cited on the impropriety of arrest for minor offences, but a NSW first-instance authority applying NSW legislation — note WA has its own statutory framework.
oneLine: Using arrest for a minor offence when a summons or court attendance notice would suffice can be an "impropriety" under s.138 Evidence Act 1995 (NSW), exposing evidence of resulting resist/assault/intimidate charges to exclusion.
whatHappened: On 25/02/1999 at Wellington, Constable ROBINS attended a disturbance involving thrown rocks. Mr CARR, moderately intoxicated, swore at ROBINS and walked away still yelling. ROBINS arrested CARR for offensive language (maximum penalty a $660 fine). CARR resisted, a melee followed, and his shirt was torn; at the police station CARR allegedly threatened Constable SMITH and SMITH's family. CARR was charged with resisting, assaulting and intimidating police. ROBINS knew CARR by name and address, had a field court attendance notice (FCAN) book in the car, and conceded he arrested rather than summonsed because it was "far quicker". The magistrate (HEILPERN LCM) held the arrest, though lawful under s.352 Crimes Act 1900, was improper, and excluded the evidence under s.138 Evidence Act 1995, dismissing the three charges. The DPP sought review under s.109(a) Justices Act 1902.
whatHeld: SMART AJ held the magistrate was entitled to find the arrest improper within s.138. Lawfulness of an arrest does not make it proper. For minor offences where the offender's name and address are known, there is no risk of flight, and no reason to think a summons would be ineffective, arrest should not be used and a summons or CAN should be issued. "Improper" is not confined to bad faith, abuse of power or intentional wrongdoing; the test is objective and Ridgeway (entrapment, serious drug offence) sets the high threshold only for serious matters. The Court accepted that evidence of further offences precipitated by an ill-advised arrest can be "obtained in consequence of an impropriety" where the offences are closely connected and at the lower end of the scale (though not where they are moderately serious to serious). The magistrate's recklessness and seriousness findings were open. However, the magistrate denied the prosecution procedural fairness by relying on a Law Society Journal article, statistics and his own court experience without alerting the prosecutor (s.144(4) Evidence Act 1995). The dismissals were quashed and the matters remitted.
whatItMeans: Treat arrest for a minor offence as a last resort. Where you know a suspect's name and address, there is no flight risk, and a summons or court attendance notice would get them to court, you should proceed by summons/CAN, not arrest — even if the arrest is technically lawful under s.352 Crimes Act 1900. If you arrest unnecessarily and the suspect then resists, assaults or threatens police, the evidence of those new offences can be excluded under s.138 because it flowed from the improper arrest. The trifecta pattern (offensive language → arrest → resist/assault) is exactly what the court is warning against. Document your reasons for choosing arrest over summons; "it was quicker" or expedience will not survive scrutiny and can be found reckless. Note the limit: where the precipitated offence is moderately serious to serious (e.g. attempted murder, GBH), that evidence will still be admitted. Consider intoxication and whether threats were idle bluster when assessing intimidate-police charges.
verdict: AWARENESS-shifting authority on charging practice — it changes how you decide between arrest and summons for minor offences, so treat the practical guidance as ACTION-relevant even though the case itself is a procedural-fairness remittal.

appealFrom: Court of Appeal of Queensland (Swaffield) and Court of Appeal of Victoria (Pavic)
outcome: Both appeals dismissed. SWAFFIELD's acquittal of arson (entered by the Queensland Court of Appeal) stood; PAVIC's murder conviction stood. Kirby J dissented in PAVIC, favouring a new trial.
weight: High Court of Australia, five-Justice bench; a leading authority on the admissibility of confessions and the unfairness and public policy discretions. Binding and frequently applied.
oneLine: Covert recordings of admissions by an undercover police officer or police agent may be excluded where the suspect, having already exercised the right to silence, has admissions actively elicited from him in derogation of his free choice to speak or stay silent.
whatHappened: Two joined appeals. SWAFFIELD was charged with arson, breaking and entering, and stealing relating to a fire at the Leichhardt Rowing Club. He twice declined to answer police questions and the original charges were dropped when no evidence was offered at committal. Months later, during an undercover drug operation, Constable MARSHALL — posing as a criminal and not cautioning SWAFFIELD — covertly recorded conversations in which SWAFFIELD admitted involvement in the arson. He was re-charged and convicted. PAVIC was suspected of the murder of Andrew ASTBURY, whose handcuffed body was found in the Yarra River. On 03/01/1995 PAVIC, on his solicitor's advice, made no comment to police and was released. Police then fitted his close friend CLANCY with a recording device. CLANCY, who feared he himself was suspected, engaged PAVIC in conversation and elicited inculpatory admissions. PAVIC was convicted of murder. Neither confession was said to be involuntary or illegally obtained.
whatHeld: The Court unified the approach to confessional evidence: admissibility turns first on voluntariness, then on reliability, then on a discretion to exclude covering unfairness to the accused and public policy. Toohey, Gaudron and Gummow JJ held the common law should be developed around the suspect's right to choose whether or not to speak; where that freedom is impugned, the court has a discretion to exclude. Adopting the Canadian approach (Hebert, Broyles), the focus is whether admissions were actively elicited by police or a police agent, rather than merely overheard. In SWAFFIELD, the undercover officer actively elicited the arson admissions in clear breach of his right to choose whether to speak — appeal dismissed (conviction stayed quashed). In PAVIC, the majority held nothing improper occurred: CLANCY was trusted by PAVIC, the admissions indicated reliability, and there was no sufficient reason to interfere — appeal dismissed. Kirby J dissented on PAVIC, holding the police exploited the relationship of trust between the two friends to circumvent PAVIC's exercised right to silence, and would have ordered a new trial.
whatItMeans: When you use undercover officers or co-operating civilians wired to record a suspect, the key legal risk is active elicitation. Merely listening to or recording spontaneous admissions is generally acceptable; questioning that is the functional equivalent of an interrogation is not, especially where the suspect has already exercised the right to silence. The distinction matters most where the operative is, or is acting as an agent of, a police officer. Be aware that targeting a suspect who has declined to answer questions — and using a disguise or a trusted friend to get what they refused to tell police — invites exclusion under the unfairness and public policy discretions. It is not unlawful to use deception, ruses or recording technology in investigations, and reliability of the admission and seriousness of the offence weigh in favour of admission. But exploiting a relationship of trust to extract a confession the suspect would not otherwise make is the line to watch. Document why questioning was conversational rather than an interrogation, and be cautious where the target has already refused to speak.
verdict: AWARENESS — this is a foundational High Court statement of principle on confession admissibility that shapes how covert recording operations are assessed, but it sets discretionary standards rather than fixed investigative rules.

outcome: Leading authority on lies as consciousness of guilt (the Edwards direction)
weight: High Court — binds all Australian courts
oneLine: A lie shows consciousness of guilt only if it is deliberate, material, and told because the truth would implicate — otherwise it just goes to credit.
whatHappened: The High Court’s statement of when an accused’s lie can be used as evidence of guilt rather than merely as a matter of credit. (Held at principle level here — facts not reproduced.)
whatHeld: A lie can be evidence of a consciousness of guilt only where it is (1) deliberate, (2) material — about a circumstance connected to the offence, and (3) told because the truth would implicate the accused. A lie failing those limbs goes only to credit, and the jury must be directed carefully so ordinary lies aren’t treated as proof of guilt.
whatItMeans: When a suspect gives a false account it can become evidence, not just bad credit — but only if it meets the three limbs. So lock the account down in free recall before you challenge: pin them to a clear, detailed version first, so a later lie is demonstrably deliberate and material.
verdict: Changes interview craft: secure the account before the challenge so a lie can carry evidential weight.

outcome: Mandatory cogent warning on disputed identification evidence
weight: High Court — binds all Australian courts
oneLine: Where identification is significant and disputed, the judge must give a cogent, specific warning about its dangers — ID evidence is inherently risky.
whatHappened: The High Court on the directions required where disputed identification evidence is significant to the case. (Held at principle level here.)
whatHeld: Where a case depends on disputed identification evidence, the trial judge must give the jury a cogent, specific warning about the special dangers of ID evidence and the factors affecting its reliability.
whatItMeans: Treat ID as fragile and process it properly — proper identification procedures, a contemporaneous note of the witness’s first description and confidence, no contamination. It will be scrutinised hard, so the integrity of how you obtained it is everything.
verdict: Drives the habit: rigorous, well-documented identification procedures.

caseName: Petty and Maiden v The Queen
outcome: No adverse inference from exercising the right to silence pre-trial
weight: High Court — binds all Australian courts
oneLine: No adverse inference may be drawn against a suspect for exercising the right to silence before trial.
whatHappened: The High Court confirmed the protection attaching to pre-trial silence. (Held at principle level here.)
whatHeld: A person’s exercise of the right to silence before trial cannot be used to draw an adverse inference of guilt; the burden remains on the State.
whatItMeans: Don’t build your case on, or invite comment on, a suspect’s silence. A no-comment interview is their right — keep proving the case from the evidence, not from the silence. (The DSS’s ‘have a rip’ is about lawful persistence within the interview, not using silence against them.)
verdict: Drives the habit: never rely on or remark on a suspect’s silence.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of New South Wales
outcome: Special leave to appeal granted; appeal dismissed.
weight: High Court of Australia, five Justices, unanimous in result. A leading and binding authority on the use of circumstantial evidence and the standard of proof for intermediate facts.
oneLine: Circumstantial cases: only indispensable intermediate facts (the "links in a chain") must be proved beyond reasonable doubt — not every item of evidence.
whatHappened: James William SHEPHERD was convicted of conspiring to import heroin into Australia. The Crown case rested on circumstantial evidence in three categories: evidence from two undercover officers who overheard Clark tell SHEPHERD to "take over the reins" of the drug organisation; evidence from accomplices granted immunity; and evidence of financial transactions said to show SHEPHERD and Clark shared the proceeds. The NSW Court of Criminal Appeal initially held the trial judge erred by not giving a so-called "Chamberlain direction" — that the jury could only infer guilt from facts proved beyond reasonable doubt. The Crown argued no such direction was required. The matter reached the High Court on the question of whether that direction must be given.
whatHeld: The High Court held that Chamberlain v The Queen [No 2] (1984) 153 CLR 521 is not authority for the proposition that a jury must be directed that they cannot use a fact to infer guilt unless that fact is proved beyond reasonable doubt. The criminal standard applies to the elements of the offence and to any intermediate fact that is an indispensable link in the chain of reasoning towards guilt — such a link must be proved beyond reasonable doubt. But where the evidence consists of "strands in a cable" rather than "links in a chain", no such direction is appropriate. Individual items of evidence used to build an inference do not each have to be proved beyond reasonable doubt; the probative force of evidence is cumulative. There is no invariable rule of law or practice that the direction be given in every circumstantial case — it is a matter for the trial judge depending on the case. SHEPHERD's case was a "cable" case: the jury only had to be satisfied, on the whole of the evidence, that no reasonable explanation consistent with innocence remained, which is what they were told. (McHugh J read Chamberlain as intending the broader proposition but agreed it is not authority requiring such a direction to a jury.)
whatItMeans: This shapes how you should think about building a circumstantial brief. Identify the indispensable links in your chain of proof — the intermediate facts that, if not established, cause the case to collapse (for example, that the accused was present, or that a substance was a particular drug). Those critical links must be capable of being proved beyond reasonable doubt, so direct your investigative effort to securing them firmly. By contrast, where guilt is inferred from many independent strands that reinforce each other (a "cable"), the strength comes from the accumulation, and no single strand needs to reach that standard on its own. The lesson: the more independent incriminating circumstances you can assemble, the more robust the case, and the less any one item must carry the whole weight. Be alert to whether your case turns on one or two pivotal facts (vulnerable) or many converging ones (stronger).
verdict: AWARENESS — this clarifies the law on jury directions and proof in circumstantial cases; it informs how you assemble and weigh evidence rather than changing a specific investigative procedure.

appealFrom: Full Court of the Supreme Court of Victoria
outcome: Appeal allowed; conviction and sentence set aside; new trial ordered.
weight: High Court of Australia, seven-member bench — the leading and binding statement of the common law of self-defence in Australia.
oneLine: The High Court restated the common law of self-defence into a single question — did the accused believe on reasonable grounds that it was necessary to do what he did — replacing the complex six-step Viro formula.
whatHappened: Fadil ZECEVIC was convicted in the Supreme Court of Victoria of murdering Harold TRIEBEL on 16/07/1983 at Ascot Vale. The two were neighbours in a block of units and had repeatedly clashed over leaving security gates open. On the day, after an argument ZECEVIC said TRIEBEL stabbed him in the chest, threatened to "blow your head off", and that he believed TRIEBEL might have a gun in his car. ZECEVIC went to his unit, fetched a gun, returned and shot TRIEBEL dead. The trial judge withdrew self-defence from the jury, finding the only open inference was that ZECEVIC did not reasonably believe he faced an unlawful attack threatening death or serious harm. The Full Court dismissed his appeal. He appealed to the High Court, which convened a seven-member bench to review the law of self-defence as stated in Viro v The Queen.
whatHeld: The Court (Wilson, Dawson and Toohey JJ, with Mason CJ, Brennan, Deane and Gaudron JJ agreeing in result) allowed the appeal, quashed the conviction and ordered a new trial. It held the elaborate six-proposition formula from Viro created difficult, confusing directions for trial judges and juries and should be abandoned. The law was restated simply: the ultimate question is whether the accused believed on reasonable grounds that it was necessary in self-defence to do what he did — if he had that belief and there were reasonable grounds for it, or the jury is left in reasonable doubt, he is acquitted. The Court confirmed self-defence has an objective element (reasonable grounds) and rejected the argument that an honest belief alone suffices. The onus remains on the Crown to disprove self-defence beyond reasonable doubt. It also held there is no rule requiring retreat as far as possible — retreat is just one circumstance to weigh — and that an original aggressor who provokes a lawful attack cannot then claim self-defence. On the facts, self-defence was squarely raised and should have been left to the jury; the trial judge wrongly substituted his own view.
whatItMeans: This is the leading common law authority on self-defence in Australia, but note WA self-defence is governed by Criminal Code (WA) s 248, not the common law — the judgment itself flags the code states operate differently. The case is still important for understanding the framework. The practical lesson for your casework: where self-defence may arise, the burden falls on the prosecution to disprove it, so build your brief to address it. Gather evidence on the accused's belief and whether it was reasonable — the nature and immediacy of any threat, proportionality of force, opportunity to retreat or break off, who started the conflict, and the accused's account. Here the prosecution's strongest point was the time gap: the accused left, armed himself, and returned. Evidence of a delay, of the accused arming himself, of an aggressor returning to a finished conflict, and of staged scenes (the prosecution alleged a knife was planted in the deceased's hand) are all critical to disproving self-defence.
verdict: AWARENESS for WA — this is foundational common law on self-defence, but WA charges run under Criminal Code (WA) s 248, so treat it as background to how courts reason rather than a directly governing test.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of the Supreme Court of Tasmania
outcome: Special leave granted; appeal allowed; Court of Criminal Appeal's order for retrial set aside; verdicts of acquittal on the 26 counts restored.
weight: High Court of Australia, five Justices — the leading authority on the limits of detention after arrest at common law.
oneLine: WILLIAMS v THE QUEEN confirms there is no power to detain an arrested person for questioning or further investigation — they must be brought before a justice as soon as practicable.
whatHappened: WILLIAMS was arrested near Scottsdale, Tasmania at about 6 a.m. on 17/05/1984 for burglary and stealing offences committed at Scottsdale (counts 27 to 29). He was taken to Launceston where, after being shown police reports, he indicated involvement in other offences across northern Tasmania (counts 1 to 26). From about 1.10 p.m. detectives conducted a series of interviews in which he confessed to all the offences; the last interview ended about 8.30 p.m. He confirmed the records to a police inspector at 9.03 p.m. and was taken before a magistrate the next day. The trial judge found WILLIAMS could have been charged with the Scottsdale matters and brought before a court by 2.15 p.m. on 17/05/1984, so his detention after that time was unlawful. The judge excluded the confessions on the 26 other counts in his discretion, the Crown offered no evidence and WILLIAMS was acquitted on those counts. The Court of Criminal Appeal allowed the Attorney-General's appeal and ordered a retrial. WILLIAMS sought special leave to the High Court.
whatHeld: The High Court granted special leave, allowed the appeal and restored the acquittals. It held that there is no power to detain an arrested person for the purpose of questioning or further investigation, whether about the offence of arrest or other offences. The statutory obligations under Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 34A(1) ('as soon as is practicable') and Criminal Code 1924 (Tas) s 303(1) ('without delay') reflect the common law: an arrested person may be detained only for the purpose of being brought before a justice as soon as reasonably practicable. Reasonable time is allowed for completing necessary inquiries, formulating and laying charges and preparing paperwork, but detention may not be prolonged to provide an opportunity for interrogation or evidence-gathering. The Court rejected the English approach in Dallison v Caffery and Holgate-Mohammed v Duke, affirming R v Iorlano. Gibbs CJ held the trial judge applied too strict a test in the discretionary exclusion (a voluntary, confirmed confession is only most exceptionally excluded for illegality), but the majority upheld the acquittals because the detention was unlawful. The Court also held the Attorney-General's appeal raised a question of mixed fact and law, not 'a question of law alone' under s 401(2)(b), so the Court of Criminal Appeal had no power to grant leave.
whatItMeans: This is a foundational authority you must respect: you cannot hold an arrested person in custody so you can question them or continue investigating — the detention clock runs only for the time reasonably needed to do the paperwork, formulate charges and get them before a justice or magistrate. Questioning is not itself unlawful, and you may question while genuinely processing someone toward court, but you must not delay bringing them before a justice in order to interview them — including about additional offences they volunteer. Once a reasonable time to charge and present has passed, continued detention is unlawful and confessions obtained during it are at risk of exclusion. Note this case predates modern WA detention regimes — your actual time limits and questioning powers come from current WA legislation, not this judgment. Treat WILLIAMS as the common law backdrop explaining why detention-for-questioning powers must be expressly authorised and time-limited by statute. Build your timeline so charging and court presentation are not held up by interviews, and document why any delay was genuinely about process, not interrogation.
verdict: AWARENESS — a landmark statement of principle on detention after arrest, but WA now has its own statutory detention and questioning regime, so check current legislation for your operative time limits.

caseName: He Kaw Teh v The Queen
appealFrom: Full Court of the Supreme Court of Victoria (Court of Criminal Appeal)
outcome: Special leave granted; appeal allowed; judgment of the Victorian Full Court set aside; matter remitted to that Court to proceed in accordance with the High Court's judgment.
weight: High Court of Australia, five-justice bench — a foundational and binding authority on mens rea in statutory offences. Frequently cited and still the leading Australian statement on the presumption of mental elements.
oneLine: The High Court held that Customs Act 1901 drug importation and possession offences require the prosecution to prove the accused knew of the existence and nature of the narcotic goods — they are not absolute offences.
whatHappened: HE KAW TEH flew from Kuala Lumpur to Melbourne on 20/10/1982. Customs found 2.788kg of heroin in a false bottom of his bag. He was charged under s 233B(1)(b) of the Customs Act 1901 (Cth) with importing heroin, and under s 233B(1)(c) with possession of imported heroin. The trial judge directed the jury that the Crown need prove no state of mind, and that the accused bore the onus of proving honest and reasonable belief (on the importation count) or reasonable excuse (on the possession count). He was convicted. The Victorian Full Court dismissed his appeal because it was bound by R v Parsons and R v Ditroia and Tucci. He sought special leave to the High Court.
whatHeld: The Court (Gibbs CJ, Mason, Wilson, Brennan and Dawson JJ) granted leave, allowed the appeal and remitted the matter. There is a strong presumption that mens rea is an element of every statutory offence, displaced only by clear words or necessary implication, and the presumption is strongest for serious, truly criminal offences. Given the gravity (life imprisonment), the Court held that on a charge under s 233B(1)(b) the prosecution must prove the accused knew he was importing narcotic goods (knowledge includes wilful blindness). On s 233B(1)(c), possession itself imports knowledge — a person does not possess narcotic goods they do not know exist, so the Crown must prove the accused knew of the existence of the goods in their control. The onus of proving knowledge rests on the prosecution beyond reasonable doubt; the accused carries only an evidential burden. R v Bush, R v Gardiner, R v Parsons and R v Ditroia and Tucci were disapproved. The trial directions were wrong; the matter was returned to the Full Court to consider whether the proviso (no substantial miscarriage) applied.
whatItMeans: For drug importation and possession matters, knowledge is a live element you must prove. It is not enough to show the accused physically carried or controlled a bag containing drugs — you must be able to establish they knew the drugs were there (and, for importation, knew they were importing narcotic goods). Build your brief around proof of knowledge. The judgments make clear knowledge can be inferred from the circumstances — concealment, weight, the nature and feel of the container, suspicious circumstances of handing over, and an absence of any convincing innocent explanation. Wilful blindness counts as knowledge: where a courier deliberately avoids inquiry despite aroused suspicions, that can satisfy the mental element. Gather evidence going to what the accused knew or deliberately ignored — records of how the bag came to them, their conduct and statements, the obviousness of the concealment. Mere custody is not the finish line.
verdict: ACTION — this changes what you must prove in drug import/possession cases: knowledge is an element, so your investigation must capture evidence of the accused's awareness or wilful blindness.

outcome: Established the discretion to exclude improperly/illegally obtained evidence
weight: High Court — binds all Australian courts
oneLine: Courts have a discretion to exclude improperly or illegally obtained evidence, weighing the public interest in convicting against the unfairness or illegality in how it was obtained.
whatHappened: The case concerned evidence obtained through an improper/unlawful exercise of police power; the High Court set out how courts decide whether to admit or exclude such evidence. (Held at principle level here.)
whatHeld: There is a public-policy discretion to exclude evidence that was illegally or improperly obtained — distinct from the unfairness discretion — balancing the public interest in convicting the guilty against the public interest in not condoning unlawful conduct by police. (Companion: R v Ireland (1970) 126 CLR 321.)
whatItMeans: Get the power right the first time. An unlawful search, entry or seizure hands the defence the Bunning v Cross argument to exclude your evidence. Record your authority for every act — which power, what grounds, when — so the balance falls your way if it’s litigated.
verdict: Drives the habit: lawful power + recorded authority for every search, entry and seizure.

court: Supreme Court of the Northern Territory
outcome: Established the Anunga Rules for interviewing Aboriginal suspects
weight: NT Supreme Court — strong persuasive weight in WA
oneLine: Guidelines for interviewing Aboriginal suspects — an interpreter where needed, a prisoner’s friend, and explaining the caution phrase by phrase with the suspect repeating it back.
whatHappened: The NT Supreme Court laid down practical guidelines (the ‘Anunga Rules’) for the fair interviewing of Aboriginal suspects. (Held at principle level here.)
whatHeld: When interviewing an Aboriginal suspect: use an interpreter where there is any doubt about language; have a prisoner’s friend present; explain the caution phrase by phrase and have the suspect say it back in their own words; take care over offers of food, drink and rest.
whatItMeans: Pilbara-critical for you. Build the Anunga Rules into every relevant interview in your patch — interpreter, prisoner’s friend, caution explained and confirmed — or the admissions are at real risk of exclusion.
verdict: Directly changes interview practice in your region — Anunga compliance, every relevant job.

appealFrom: Supreme Court of South Australia (Court of Criminal Appeal)
outcome: Special leave to appeal refused with costs; the order for a new trial stood. (Editor's note: on retrial IRELAND was convicted of murder on 27/08/1970 and his further appeal was dismissed.)
weight: High Court of Australia, unanimous (McTiernan, Windeyer, Owen and Walsh JJ all agreeing with Barwick CJ). A leading authority on the public-policy discretion to exclude improperly obtained evidence.
oneLine: The High Court refused the Crown special leave, confirming that evidence obtained by unlawful or unfair means is not automatically excluded but is subject to a judicial discretion weighing the public interest in conviction against protection from unlawful treatment.
whatHappened: A female hotel licensee was stabbed to death in Adelaide on 16/11/1969. IRELAND, who admitted being at the hotel, became the suspect after his footprints and a cigarette butt were found near the body. On 22/11/1969 police interrogated him; after a caution he said he did not wish to answer further questions, but the officer persisted with a series of questions to which he repeatedly said "I don't wish to answer". Police later compelled him to have his scratched right hand photographed, and a doctor examined the hand, linking the marks to a knife with a broken handle. The trial judge admitted the questioning evidence, the photographs and the medical evidence over objection. IRELAND was convicted of murder. The South Australian Court of Criminal Appeal set aside the conviction by majority and ordered a new trial. The Attorney-General of South Australia sought special leave to appeal to the High Court.
whatHeld: Special leave refused; an appeal would have failed. The High Court held that evidence of relevant facts or things obtained by unlawful or unfair acts is not for that reason alone inadmissible — whether the unlawfulness derives from common law or statute — but whenever such unlawfulness or unfairness appears the judge has a discretion to reject the evidence and must consider its exercise, weighing the public need to convict offenders against the public interest in protecting the individual from unlawful and unfair treatment ("convictions obtained by the aid of unlawful or unfair acts may be obtained at too high a price"). On the facts: the account of the persisted questioning after the caution was irrelevant and inadmissible because the accused's failure to answer after warning could not amount to an admission. Compelling a person to submit to photography for a purpose other than identification was beyond police power at common law and under s. 81 of the Police Offences Act 1953-1960 (SA); the photographs and dependent medical evidence should have been excluded as a matter of discretion, the trial judge having failed to exercise any discretion at all. An aggregate of faults, none fatal alone, can together mean the trial miscarried.
whatItMeans: This is a foundational case on illegally or improperly obtained evidence. What it means for your casework: gathering evidence unlawfully will not automatically destroy a brief, but it puts the evidence at the mercy of a judicial discretion that weighs your conduct against the accused's rights — so do it properly the first time. Once a suspect has been cautioned and says he does not wish to answer, stop interrogating; persisting is improper and his silence proves nothing and is inadmissible. You cannot compel a suspect to be photographed or examined for any purpose beyond establishing identity unless a statute authorises it and you meet its conditions — here breaching the procedural safeguards denied the accused his own medical examination and got the evidence excluded. An officer's personal opinion that the suspect committed the crime is irrelevant and prejudicial. Confirm modern WA equivalents for compelled photography, forensic procedures and silence before relying on this.
verdict: AWARENESS-level but practically important: it does not change a single procedure but sets the discretionary framework that governs whether your unlawfully obtained evidence survives — investigate lawfully so it does.

outcome: Confession admissible only if voluntary (basal voluntariness)
weight: High Court — binds all Australian courts
oneLine: A confession is admissible only if it is voluntary — not obtained by inducement, threat, or a will that has been overborne.
whatHappened: The High Court’s statement of the voluntariness rule governing the admissibility of confessions. (Held at principle level here.)
whatHeld: A confession is admissible only if voluntary — made in the exercise of a free choice, not obtained by inducement, threat, or oppression that overbears the will (‘basal voluntariness’).
whatItMeans: Voluntariness is the whole ball game in an interview. Caution properly, offer no inducements, apply no oppression — and beware over-repetition that ‘overbears’ a suspect (the lesson the DSS drove home). If voluntariness fails, the admission is gone, however true.
verdict: Drives interview conduct: a clean caution, no inducements, no oppression — protect voluntariness.

outcome: Conviction quashed; appeal allowed — the prosecution bears the burden of proving guilt.
weight: House of Lords (UK) — foundational; the presumption-of-innocence cornerstone, applied across the common-law world including WA.
oneLine: The “golden thread” of the criminal law — the prosecution must prove the accused’s guilt; the accused need not prove innocence (subject only to insanity and statutory exceptions).
whatHappened: Reginald WOOLMINGTON, aged 21, was charged with murdering his estranged 17-year-old wife. He claimed the killing was an accident — that he only meant to frighten her into returning to him by threatening suicide with a gun, but it went off. The trial judge directed the jury that, once the killing was proved, it was for WOOLMINGTON to prove it was an accident. He was convicted and sentenced to death; the Court of Criminal Appeal upheld the conviction; the House of Lords quashed it.
whatHeld: Viscount SANKEY LC stated the “golden thread”: it is the duty of the prosecution to prove the prisoner’s guilt, subject only to the defence of insanity and to any statutory exception. If, on the whole of the case, there is a reasonable doubt — raised by either the prosecution’s or the prisoner’s evidence — the prosecution has not made out its case and the accused is entitled to an acquittal. The accused never has to prove innocence.
whatItMeans: This is the cornerstone you build every brief on: the State proves guilt — the suspect proves nothing. Assemble your evidence to discharge that burden to the criminal standard, and never structure a case (or an interview) on the premise that a suspect must explain themselves or account for their innocence. The main carve-out you’ll meet on the job is insanity / unsoundness of mind, which the accused must raise and prove (see Stefanski), plus specific statutory reverse-onus provisions.
verdict: AWARENESS — the foundational statement of the prosecution’s burden; it shapes how you build and think about every brief, even though it lays down no operational procedure.
flags: Full speech not yet in the app — the verbatim ‘golden thread’ ratio is shown; add the complete [1935] AC 462 speech when available.

outcome: Established the rule of practice: put your case to a witness you intend to contradict
weight: UK House of Lords — long-accepted rule of practice in Australian courts
oneLine: If a party intends to contradict a witness, it must put that case to the witness in cross-examination and give them a chance to respond.
whatHappened: The classic statement of the rule of fair cross-examination. (Held at principle level here.)
whatHeld: A party who intends to contradict or impeach a witness must put that case to the witness in cross-examination, so the witness has an opportunity to explain — otherwise it is unfair to later invite the tribunal to disbelieve them.
whatItMeans: In the box the rule cuts both ways: the defence must put their case to you, and your evidence will be tested against it. Your statement and notes must be complete and contemporaneous — a tight record is your armour when your account is challenged.
verdict: Court-craft to understand: complete, contemporaneous notes are your protection when cross-examined.
